coredata.dc:title: A metal-organic framework functionalized CaO2-based cascade nanoreactor induces synergistic cuproptosis/ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload-mediated mitochondrial damage for enhanced sono-chemodynamic immunotherapy

coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Cong
Liu, Kairui
Gao, Xiaoning
Kang, Hanmeixuan
Xie, Weijie
Chang, Jin
Yin, Linling
Kang, Jun
coredata.dc:description:
Cuproptosis is an emerging form of programmed cell death and shows enormous prospect in cancer treatment. Excessive generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), metal ion accumulation, and the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle collapse are pivotal elements in the triggering of cell death via mitochondrial pathways. Herein, a cascade nanoreactor CaCuZC has been constructed by incorporating nanosonosensitizer IR780 carbon dots (IR780 CD) and calcium peroxide (CaO2) into metal-organic frameworks (MOF) for synergistic cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload mediated immunotherapy. Within tumor cells, CaCuZC dissociates into CaO2, Cu2+and sonosensitizer IR780 CD. The decomposition of CaO2 could generate H2O2 to strengthen the Cu2+-based chemodynamic therapy and Ca2+overload induces amplified intracellular oxidative stress, thus leading to mitochondrial dysfunction. As a result, the combination of Cu2+and Ca2+ overload together induce cascade mitochondrial damage. Moreover, the sonosensitizer IR780 CD generates ROS under ultrasound irradiation to amplify intracellular oxidative stress. In addition, the overloaded Cu2+ released from CaCuZC leads to the aggregation of lipoylated protein dihydrolipoamide S-acetyltransferase, thus resulting in cuproptosis. Furthermore, ferroptosis could been concomitantly induced by CaCuZC with intracellular glutathione (GSH) consumption and lipid peroxidation (LPO) accumulation. The cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced synergistic therapy also activates robust immunogenic cell death. CaCuZC enhances the infiltration and activation of tumor-specific cytotoxic T cells to transform a “cold” tumor into a “hot” tumor, activating the anti-tumor immune response. This study provides a cascade of mitochondrial damage strategy for triggering cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced immunotherapy and achieving improved therapeutic effects.
Statement of significance To improve the efficacy of tumor immunotherapy, a cascade nanoreactor CaCuZC was successfully constructed based on a self-assembly strategy for cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload mediated immunotherapy. Upon decomposition within the acidic and GSH-overexpressing tumor microenvironment, CaCuZC released CaO2 and Cu2+ and sonosensitizer IR780 CD. The CaO2 further produced H2O2/O2 and Ca2+ in a weakly acidic environment to strengthen the Cu2+-based CDT and IR780 CD-mediated SDT, respectively. The overload copper ions not only led to cuproptosis, but also efficiently induced ferroptosis. The cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced synergistic therapy also activates robust immunogenic cell death. This study presents a cascade of mitochondrial damage strategy for cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced immunotherapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation and therapeutic application of CaCuZC. (A) Construction diagram of CaCuZC; (B) Schematic illustration of CaCuZC for calcium overload and cuproptosis–ferroptosis enhanced immunotherapy.

Characterization of CaO2@CuZIF/CD NPs (CaCuZC). TEM image of (a) CaO2 NP, (b) IR780CD and (c) of CaCuZC. (d) Elemental mapping of CaCuZC. Scale bars: 100 nm. (e) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution and (f) zeta potential of CaO2 NP and CaCuZC. (g) UV–vis adsorption spectra of IR780CD, CaO2@CuZIF and CaO2@CuZIF/CD. (h) XPS survey spectra of CaCuZC. High-resolution XPS scan of (i) Cu 2p, (j) Ca 2p.

Sonodynamic/chemodynamic properties of CaCuZC. (a) Time-dependent oxidation of DPBF indicating 1O2 generation by US-activated CaCuZC. (b) Time-dependent 1O2 generation by CaCuZC under US irradiation using SOSG probe. (c) CaCuZC-involved concentration-related GSH depletion. (d) UV/Vis absorption peaks and photograph (inset) of MB aqueous solution in different groups. (e) MB degraded by CaCuZC-mediated Fenton-like reaction at different time points. (f) UV/Vis absorption peaks of MB aqueous solution treated with different concentrations of CaCuZC in the presence of H2O2 (10 mM). (g) UV/Vis absorption peaks of MB aqueous solution treated with different concentrations of H2O2 in the presence of CaCuZC. (h) UV–vis spectra and photograph (inset) of TMB solution in different groups. (i) The ability of CaCuZC and H2O2 to produce •OH at different time points measured based on TMB experiment. (j) UV–vis absorption peaks of TMB aqueous solution treated with different concentrations of CaCuZC in the presence of H2O2 (10 mM). (k) ESR spectra for detection of 1O2 in the presence of TEMP. (l) ESR spectra for detection of hydroxyl radicals in the presence of DMPO.

In vitro therapeutic effects of CaCuZC. (a) Viability of 4T1 cells after being treated with CaZC without or with US irradiation (n = 5, mean ± s.d.). (b) Viability of 4T1 cells after being treated with CaCuZC without or with US irradiation (n = 5, mean ± s.d.). (c) Relative cell viability of 4T1 cells after different treatments (n = 5, mean ± s.d.). (d) CLSM images of 4T1 cells incubated with CaCuZC for 1, 3, and 5 h Scale bar is 20 μm.(e) Intracellular ROS level of 4T1 cells after different treatments. Scale bar: 200 μm. (f) CLSM images of 4T1 cells stained with Calcein-AM and PI after various treatments. Scale bar: 200 μm. (g) Flow cytometry analysis of early and late apoptosis of 4T1 cells exposed to different treatments.

Studies on the antitumor mechanism of CaCuZC. (a) The proposed schematic diagram of CaCuZC-induced cuproptosis–ferroptosis. (b) The relative GSH contents in 4T1 cells after various treatments. (n = 3, mean ± s.d.). (c-d) Western blot analysis on the expressions of LIAS, FDX1, GPX4. (e) DLAT immunofluorescence images of cells in different groups. Scale bar: 50 μm. (f) CLSM images of C11-BODIPY-stained 4T1 cells in different groups. Scale bar: 200 μm. (g) CLSM images of FerroOrange-stained 4T1 cells in different groups. Scale bar: 200 μm.

CaCuZC-mediated cascade mitochondrial damage and immunogenic cell death. (a) CLSM images of free calcium production in 4T1 cells after receiving different treatments. Scale bar: 50 μm. (b) Flow cytometric analysis of free calcium generation in 4T1 cells after different treatment. (c) CLSM image of 4T1 cells with JC-10 after various treatments. Scale bar: 200 μm. (d) Bio-TEM images of cells treated with cell medium (control) and CaCuZC+US. Scale bar: 5 μm and 500 nm. Mitochondria are indicated by orange arrows. (e) Immunofluorescence CRT staining images of 4T1 cells after different treatments. Extracellular HMGB1 levels(f) and ATP levels (g) for 4T1 cells in PBS-, CaZC- and CaCuZC-treated groups without or with US treatment.

Transcriptome sequencing analysis of control and treated cells. (a) Volcano plots indicate significant genetic alterations in the control and CaCuZC+US groups. (b) Distribution of genes related to apoptosis on heat map. (c) GO enrichment analysis of differential genes between control and CaCuZC+US groups. (d) KEGG enrichment analysis of differential pathway between control and CaCuZC+US groups.

Antitumor effect of CaCuZC in vivo. (a) Schematic illustration of in vivo therapeutic protocols. (b) Tumor volume changes in the mice receiving indicated treatments every 2 days (n = 5, mean ± s.d.). (c) Average weight of tumors harvested from 4T1 tumor-bearing mice in different groups after the 14-day treatment period (n = 5). Statistical significance was calculated by one-way ANOVA. (d) Tumor inhibition rate analysis of different groups (n = 5). (e) Histological images of tumor slices stained with H&E, antigen Ki-67 and TUNEL after treatments. (scale bar: 100 μm). Quantitative analysis of TNF-α (f) and IL2 (g) secretion in the serum of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice after different treatments (n = 3).

In vivo antitumor immune responses. Flow cytometry (a) and the relative quantification (e) of DC maturation (CD11C+CD80+CD86+) in 4T1 tumors after various treatments. Flow cytometry (b) and the relative quantification (f) of DC maturation in tumor-draining lymph nodes after various treatments. Flow cytometry (c) and (g) quantitative analysis of tumor infiltrating CD8+ T lymphocytes (CD45+CD3+CD4+CD8a+) within 4T1 tumors. Flow cytometry (d) and (h) quantitative analysis of CD8+ T lymphocytes within spleens.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cuproptosis is an emerging form of programmed cell death and shows enormous prospect in cancer treatment. Excessive generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), metal ion accumulation, and the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle collapse are pivotal elements in the triggering of cell death via mitochondrial pathways. Herein, a cascade nanoreactor CaCuZC has been constructed by incorporating nanosonosensitizer IR780 carbon dots (IR780 CD) and calcium peroxide (CaO2) into metal-organic frameworks (MOF) for synergistic cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload mediated immunotherapy. Within tumor cells, CaCuZC dissociates into CaO2, Cu2+and sonosensitizer IR780 CD. The decomposition of CaO2 could generate H2O2 to strengthen the Cu2+-based chemodynamic therapy and Ca2+overload induces amplified intracellular oxidative stress, thus leading to mitochondrial dysfunction. As a result, the combination of Cu2+and Ca2+ overload together induce cascade mitochondrial damage. Moreover, the sonosensitizer IR780 CD generates ROS under ultrasound irradiation to amplify intracellular oxidative stress. In addition, the overloaded Cu2+ released from CaCuZC leads to the aggregation of lipoylated protein dihydrolipoamide S-acetyltransferase, thus resulting in cuproptosis. Furthermore, ferroptosis could been concomitantly induced by CaCuZC with intracellular glutathione (GSH) consumption and lipid peroxidation (LPO) accumulation. The cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced synergistic therapy also activates robust immunogenic cell death. CaCuZC enhances the infiltration and activation of tumor-specific cytotoxic T cells to transform a “cold” tumor into a “hot” tumor, activating the anti-tumor immune response. This study provides a cascade of mitochondrial damage strategy for triggering cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced immunotherapy and achieving improved therapeutic effects.

To improve the efficacy of tumor immunotherapy, a cascade nanoreactor CaCuZC was successfully constructed based on a self-assembly strategy for cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload mediated immunotherapy. Upon decomposition within the acidic and GSH-overexpressing tumor microenvironment, CaCuZC released CaO2 and Cu2+ and sonosensitizer IR780 CD. The CaO2 further produced H2O2/O2 and Ca2+ in a weakly acidic environment to strengthen the Cu2+-based CDT and IR780 CD-mediated SDT, respectively. The overload copper ions not only led to cuproptosis, but also efficiently induced ferroptosis. The cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced synergistic therapy also activates robust immunogenic cell death. This study presents a cascade of mitochondrial damage strategy for cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+overload-enhanced immunotherapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer cell survival and proliferation are largely dependent on the homeostasis of subcellular organelles [1,2]. Generally, tumor tissue exhibits an enhanced metabolic and antioxidant stress state [3]. Action sites based on reactive oxygen species (ROS) therapy locate within organelles and personalized strategies need to be developed to precisely modulate intracellular suborganelles. Mitochondria plays a crucial role in redox homeostasis, tricarboxylic acid cycle and apoptosis in tumor cells [4]. As a central hub for metal ion metabolism, mitochondrial disruption exacerbates the oxidative stress that causes cancer cell death. Therefore, targeting mitochondria to amplify the ion-disrupting effects is an ideal strategy for cancer therapy.

Metal ion regulation-mediated tumor therapy is an emerging approach, which relies on direct or indirect alteration of intracellular metal ion concentrations for tumor suppression and immune modulation [5]. Ionic homeostasis in normal cells is tightly and precisely regulated for cell survival, metabolism and immunity. However, due to the immortalization and immune escape properties of tumor cells, intracellular metal ion-related signaling pathways are remodeled, resulting in tumor cells being more sensitive to metal ion regulation.

The transitional metal copper is an essential trace element related to cell proliferation and death pathways. Cuproptosis is a copper-dependent nonapoptotic cell death type [6,7]. Unlike other known pathways such as apoptosis, pyroptosis, and ferroptosis, cuproptosis relies on copper accumulation in cells. The lethal mechanism of cuproptosis involves disruption of specific mitochondrial metabolic enzymes. The Fe-S cluster protein ferredoxin (FDX1) would reduce Cu2+ to Cu+, causing the aggregation of lipoylated proteins (e.g., dihydrolipoamide S-acetyltransferase, DLAT) and the loss of Fe-S cluster proteins, which leads to proteotoxic stress and ultimately cell death [8–12]. Ferroptosis is a form of programmed cell death characterized by the iron-dependent accumulation of lipid peroxidation (LPO) to lethal levels [13–16]. Cu2+ could also be reduced to Cu+ by GSH, which downregulates glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) expression, leads to excess LPO enrichment, and ultimately induces ferroptosis. Besides, intracellular Cu2+ could also provide chemodynamic therapy (CDT), which is an emerging therapeutic method that transforms endogenous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) into cytotoxic hydroxyl radicals (•OH) via Fenton or Fenton-like reactions to kill tumor cells [17–22]. Although the H2O2 level in the tumor cells is relatively higher than in normal cells, the therapeutic efficacy of CDT is still limited by H2O2 level in tumor cells. Therefore, the strategy of increasing the concentration of intracellular H2O2 can effectively enhance the therapeutic efficacy.

Calcium ions (Ca2+) is essential for the regulation of many cellular functions and act as second messengers in signal transduction pathways [23,24]. Once intracellular Ca2+ levels exceed the cellular tolerance, oxidative stress, mitochondrial damage, and apoptosis can occur [25–28]. Relevant studies demonstrate that Ca2+ overload in tumor cells effectively induces immunogenic cell death (ICD) to trigger the release of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), which can recruite antigen presenting cells and promote dendritic cells maturation, enhancing tumor cell immunogenicity and activating tumor-specific immune responses [29–35]. During ICD process, the release of DAMPs which consist of calreticulin (CRT), high mobility group protein B1 (HMGB1) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP), promotes the maturation of dendritic cells and the proliferation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes, thus activating an anti-tumor immune response [36].

Sonodynamic therapy (SDT) is a noninvasive therapeutic strategy which uses ultrasound (US)-triggering sonosensitizers to produce toxic ROS to induce cell apoptosis or necrosis [37–39]. SDT has been recognized as an emerging noninvasive cancer therapeutic method due to benefits of deep tissue penetration, minimal damage to healthy tissues and high biosafety [40–47]. The sonosensitizers are briefly activated from the ground state to the excited state under US activation, and then transfers energy to surrounding H2O or O2 molecules to generate ROS. The representative organic molecule sonosensitizers such as IR780 iodides, indocyanine green (ICG), and chlorin e6 (Ce6) exhibit poor SDT performance due to some drawbacks including poor chemical stability, low bioavailability, and limited tumor accumulation [48,49]. To address these drawbacks, carbon dots (CD) material has been developed for SDT using organic molecule sonosensitizers as precursor [50,51]. This strategy shows obvious advantages. The chemical stability of carbon dots are much higher than that of organic molecule sonosensitizer. Then, the carbon dots could produce more ROS than corresponding molecules as raw materials at the same concentration. Besides, the conversion of organic molecules to carbon dots can reduce the toxicity considerably, so that higher concentration of sonosensitizer can be applied to the treatment.

In this study, a multifunctional CaO2@CuZIF/CD (CaCuZC) nanoreactor was constructed to induce a cascade of mitochondrial damage by bimetallic ions for cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload mediated immunotherapy (Scheme 1 ). To construct the CaCuZC, a self-assembly method was employed to integrate the copper/zinc metal-organic framework (CuZIF), nanosonosensitizer IR780 carbon dots (IR780 CD), and calcium peroxide (CaO2). To increase biocompatibility, CaCuZC surface was modified with hyaluronate acid (HA). In GSH-rich and acidic intracellular microenvironment, CaCuZC could release Cu2+, IR780 CD and CaO2 cores, and the exposed CaO2 further generated Ca2+and H2O2/O2 under the acidic environments, strengthening the Cu2+-mediated Fenton-like reaction. The intracellular Cu2+ accumulation would lead to DLAT aggregation and result in cuproptosis. Cu2+ could consume the intracellular GSH, which can scavenge cytotoxic ROS to maintain the physiological activity of tumor cells, and be transformed into Cu+to catalyze the production of hydroxyl radicals from H2O2. The sonosensitizer IR780 CD generated ROS under US irradiation to amplify oxidative stress, enhanced by O2 from CaO2 decomposition. Besides, intracellular Ca2+ overload also caused mitochondrial damage and further enhanced oxidative stress in tumor cells. The boosted intracellular ROS, combined with GSH depletion, efficiently induced ferroptosis in cancer cells via GPX4 inactivation and LPO enrichment. After synergistic antitumor therapy directly killed tumor cells, the released DAMPs from dying tumor cells could provoke robust antitumor immunity, which converted the cold tumor immune microenvironment (TIM) to a hot TIM. This study introduces a promising strategy for ion-triggered cell death, through a cascade of mitochondrial impairment triggered by calcium overload and cuproptosis–ferroptosis, offering substantial prospects for cancer immunotherapy.

Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2•2H2O), copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2•3H2O), 2-methylimidazole, IR-780 iodide, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), hyaluronic acid (HA), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 5,5-dimethylpyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry, Co., Ltd. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2•6H2O), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) and ammonia(28–30 %) were purchased from Tianjin Jiangtian Metallurgy, Co., Ltd. Singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) was purchased from MeilunBio, Co., Ltd. Methylene blue (MB) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and 5,5′-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), glutathione (GSH) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. RPMI 1640 medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25 % trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8), Hoechst 33,342, 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), Calcein-AM/PI kit, Annexin V FITC/PI kit, Fluo-3AM probe, JC-10 probe, C11-BODIPY581/591, FerroOrange, ATP Content Assay Kits and GSH Content Assay Kits were purchased from Solarbio. Calcium Colorimetric Assay Kit was purchased from Beyotime. Anti-DLAT, anti-FDX1, anti-LIAS and anti-GPX4 antibodies was obtained from Proteintech. FITC anti-mouse CD11c, PE anti-mouse CD86, APC anti-mouse CD80, FITC anti-mouse CD3, PE anti-mouse CD8a, PE/Cyanine7 anti-mouse CD45 and APC anti-mouse CD4 antibody were purchased from BioLegend.

CaO2 nanoparticles were synthesized through the wet chemistry method. CaCl2 aqueous solution (1 mL, 2 mol l-1) was added to 60 mL of absolute methanol under vigorous stirring at room temperature. Next, 0.3 mL of H2O2 (30 %) dropwise added and stirred for 30 min under magnetic stirring. The NH3•H2O (0.4 mL, 28 %) was added and stirred for another 10 min. The solution gradually changed from colourless and transparent to milky white. The resulting product was collected via centrifugation (9000 rpm) and subsequently washed with methanol for three times. The achieved CaO2 NPs were stored at 4 °C.

Briefly, IR-780 iodide (10 mg) was dispersed in ethanol (30 mL) with ultrasonication for 20 min. The solution was transferred into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h After cooling to room temperature, the obtained IR780 carbon dots (IR780 CD) solution was concentrated by rotary evaporation to obtain 2 mL of IR780 CD solution (5 mg ml−1).

CaO2@CuZIF/CD NPs (CaCuZC) were synthesized according to the following protocol. Under magnetic stirring, Zn(NO3)2•6H2O methanol solution (4 mL, 0.4 M) and Cu(NO3)2•6H2O methanol solution (2 mL, 0.2 M) were added into 14 mL methanol containing CaO2 (200 mg) and IR780 carbon dots (20 mg). To the above solution, 20 mL of methanol containing 2-methylimidazole (1.32 g) was added dropwise and stirred for 1 h After that, as-obtained product was separated by centrifugation (8000 rpm), washed with methanol. The obtained CaCuZC was added into 20 mL of PBS containing hyaluronic acid (HA) and stirred for 9 h Finally, the product was purified by centrifugation (10,000 rpm) and washed with deionized water for further usage. For the preparation of CaO2@ZIF/CD NPs (CaZC), copper nitrate was not added during the synthesis process, and the others were the same as the above steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IR780 CD and Ca2+ released from CaCuZC
ce:section.ce:para: 50 mg of CaCuZC was dispersed in 5 mL of PBS at pH 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4, respectively, and the supernatant was collected at time points 2, 4, 6, 12 and 24 h, respectively. The supernatant Ca2+ concentration was measured using a calcium ion assay kit. The concentration of supernatant IR780 CD was calculated from the linear relationship between IR780 CD concentration and absorbance.

Using Ti(SO4)2 as an H2O2 indicator, its color changes from colorless to yellow when it reacts specifically with H2O2. Different concentrations of H2O2 (125, 250, 500, 750, 1000, 1250, 1500 and 2000 μM) were added to Ti(SO4)2 solution (2 mL, 0.25 mg mL-1) and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. The standard curves of absorbance versus H2O2 concentration were plotted according to the absorbance at 410 nm. 50 mg of CaCuZC was dispersed in 5 mL of PBS at pH 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4, respectively, and the supernatant was taken at time points 2, 4, 6, 12 and 24 h After adding Ti(SO4)2 solution, absorbance at 410 nm was measured and the H2O2 concentration was calculated on the basis of the standard curve.

The consumption GSH was measured using 5,5′-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) as a probe. Briefly, 2 mL of DTNB solution (0.5 mM, PBS=5.0) was mixed with GSH at different concentrations and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded to draw the standard curve between GSH concentration and absorbance at 412 nm. To detect GSH depletion by CaCuZC, 6-group GSH (1.0 mM, 2 mL, PBS=5.0) solutions were mixed with CaCuZC at different concentrations (0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0 and 1.25 mg mL-1), respectively. After 30 min, DTNB ethanol solution (0.1 M, 50 μL) was added. After 5 min, the solution was diluted 10-fold and absorbance was measured by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SDT efficacy of CaCuZC upon US irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sonodynamic properties, the generation of singlet oxygen was measured by molecular probe 1,3- diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF). CaCuZC (50 μL, 10 mg mL-1) and DPBF (10 μL, 10 mM) in DMSO solution were added to 2 mL of PBS (pH = 5.0), and then exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for different durations. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixture was measured after US irradiation (1∼6 min), respectively. DMSO solution of DPBF (20 μL, 10 mM) was added to 2 mL of PBS (pH = 5.0) as control group, and then exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for different durations. Then, the mixture was measured by

UV–vis spectrophotometer. In addition, singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) was used to measured singlet oxygen (1O2) production. CaCuZC (50 μL, 10 mg mL-1) was added to 2 mL of PBS (pH = 5.0), and then 10 μL of SOSG (0.5 mM) was added. Next, the mixed solution was irradiated with US (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for different durations. Besides, SOSG (10 μL, 0.5 mM) was added in 2 mL of PBS solution (pH = 5.0) as a control group, and then exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for different durations. The fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Ex/Em: 488/525 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of •OH generation by CaCuZC-mediated Fenton-like reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of hydroxyl radicals was detected using methylene blue (MB) probe. CaCuZC (20 μL, 10 mg mL-1) and MB (20 μL, 1 mg mL-1) were added to 2 mL of GSH (10 mM, PBS=5.0), reacted for 20 min, and then H2O2 (20 μL, 1 M) was added, and the decrease of MB absorbance was detected by UV–vis absorption spectrometry. The absorption value was recorded every 3 min for 30 min. The effect of different concentrations of H2O2 or CaCuZC on the production of hydroxyl radicals was investigated. CaCuZC (20 μL, 10 mg mL-1) and MB (20 μL, 1 mg mL-1) were added to 2 mL of GSH (10 mM, pH=5.0) solution. After 20 min, different concentrations of H2O2 (0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, and 10 mM) were added. After 10 min, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. Next, CaCuZC (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 μg mL-1) and MB (20 μL, 1 mg mL-1) were added to 2 mL of GSH (10 mM, pH=5.0) solution, reacted for 20 min, and then H2O2 (20 μL, 1 M) was added. After 10 min, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded.

In addition, the generation of hydroxyl radicals was detected using 3,3′,5,5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) probe. As follows, CaCuZC (50 μL, 10 mg mL-1) was added to 2 mL of NaAc-HAc buffer (pH=4.5) containing GSH (10 mM) and TMB (0.5 mM), reacted for 20 min, and then H2O2 (20 μL, 1 M) was added. The absorption spectrum of TMB at 650 nm was recorded every 2 min for 20 min. Following a similar approach, the effects of various concentrations of H2O2 (0–10 mM) or CaCuZC (0–120 μg mL-1) on hydroxyl radical production were also investigated.

The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra in conjunction with 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, the trapping agent for 1O2) and 5,5-dimethylpyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, the rapping agent for •OH) was employed to detect the generated ROS. For 1O2 evaluation, CaCuZC (50 μL, 10 mg mL-1) and trapping agent TEMP were added to 2 mL PBS (pH=5.0). The US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) was performed for 3 min. The 1O2 generation was detected by ESR spectrometer. For •OH evaluation, CaCuZC (20 μL, 10 mg mL-1) were added to 2 mL of GSH (10 mM, pH=5.0).Next, H2O2 (20 μL, 1 M) and trapping agent DMPO was added after 20 min. The •OH generation was also detected by ESR spectrometer.

The cellular uptake efficiency of CaCuZC was evaluated by tracing the fluorescence intensity of RhB-labeled CaCuZC. Specifically, 4T1 cells were seeded in confocal-well culture plates (1.0 × 105 cells per well). The fluorescent dye rhodamine B-labeled CaCuZC (30 μg mL-1) was added and co-incubated with the cells for 1, 3 and 5 h The non-endocytosed material was washed off with PBS and stained with Hoechst 33,342 for 10 min. Finally, 4T1 cells were imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM).

The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay was used to evaluated the in vitro cytotoxicity of CaZC and CaCuZC. In brief, 4T1 cells (0.5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plate overnight, and then treated with RPMI 1640 containing CaZC (0, 7.5, 15, 30, 60 and 120 μg mL-1) or CaCuZC (0, 7.5, 15, 30, 60 and 120 μg mL-1) for 12 h The cells were treated with US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) at the 4th hour for 5 min. After removing the medium, 100 μL of RPMI 1640 containing CCK-8 was added to each well. After 3 h, the absorbance at 450 nm was recorded by microplate reader to assesse cell viability.

The reactive oxygen probe 2′,7′- dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was used to detect intracellular ROS levels. 4T1 cells were seeded into 12 well plates (5 × 104 per well) and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h After that, the cells were sequentially treated with different samples for 4 h: (1) control; (2) US; (3) CaZC (60.0 μg mL-1); (4) CaZC+US(60.0 μg mL-1); (5) CaCuZC (60.0 μg mL-1); and (6) CaCuZC+US (60.0 μg mL-1). Next, the cells were exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for 5 min. After the residual nanomaterials were removed, 0.5 mL of serum-free medium containing Hoechst 33,342 and DCFH-DA was added to each well and incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. After the medium was removed, the cells were washed with PBS and the images were acquired by CLSM.

4T1 cells were seeded into 12 well plates (5 × 104 per well) and incubated for 24 h Then, the cells were sequentially treated with different samples: (1) control; (2) US; (3) CaZC (60.0 μg mL-1); (4) CaZC+US (60.0 μg mL-1); (5) CaCuZC (60.0 μg mL-1); and (6) CaCuZC+US(60.0 μg mL-1). The cells were exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) at the 4th hour for 5 min. After 12 h, 4T1 cells were co-stained with Calcein-AM/PI and intracellular fluorescence was examined by CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis assay
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were inoculated in 6-well plates and cultured overnight. After that, the cells were sequentially treated with the medium containing: (1) control; (2) US; (3) CaZC; (4) CaZC+US; (5) CaCuZC; and (6) CaCuZC+US with specified concentrations. 4T1 cells were treated with US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) at the 4th hour for 5 min. After 12 h, cells were collected and washed two times repeatedly with PBS, and cells were resuspended with the binding solution. For each group, 5 μL of Annexin V-FITC was added into 100 μL of cells (1 × 105 cells) and incubated for 10 min at room temperature, then 5 μL of PI was added and incubated for 5 min. Each group was added 400 μL PBS to fix the volume to 500 μL. The cell apoptosis caused by different treatment were analyzed by flow cytometry.

Intracellular calcium level was detected using Fluo-3AM fluorescent probe (Ex: 488 nm, Em: 520 nm). 4T1 cells were seeded into 12 well plates (5 × 104 per well) and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h The cells were sequentially treated with the medium containing: (1) control; (2) US; (3) CaZC; (4) CaZC+US; (5) CaCuZC; and (6) CaCuZC+US with specified concentrations. After 5 h, the cells were exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for 5 min. Then, cells were washed with Hanks buffer and further treated with Fluo-3AM working solution (2.5 μM) for 20 min. Then, cells were washed with HEPES buffer three times. Hanks buffer containing 1 % FBS was added, and incubation was continued for another 20 min. The stained cells were imaged using CLSM. Besides, stained cells were analyzed using a flow cytometer.

To measure the changes in mitochondrial membrane potential, 4T1 cells were seeded into 12 well plates (5 × 104 per well) and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h After that, the cells were sequentially treated with the medium containing: (1) control; (2) US; (3) CaZC; (4) CaZC+US; (5) CaCuZC; and (6) CaCuZC+US with specified concentrations. After 4 h, the cells were exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for 5 min. After another incubation for 8 h, cells were stained with JC-10 working solution for 20 min. Finally, fluorescent images of the diverse groups were captured by CLSM.

4T1 cells were seeded into 12-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well for 24 h Then the cells were treated with different formulations. After 24 h, the amount of GSH were determined using GSH assay kit according to the manufacturer's instructions.

4T1 cells were seeded into 12-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well for 24 h When the cell density reached 80 %, the cells were treated with Control, US, CaZC, CaZC+US, CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US, and incubated for another 12 h After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with LPO probe C11-BODIPY581/591 working solution (5 μM) at 37 °C for 20 min. After washing with PBS, intracellular LPO was observed with CLSM.

4T1 cells were seeded into 12-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well for 24 h The cells were then treated with Control, US, CaZC, CaZC+US, CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US for 6 h After that, cells were stained with Hoechst for 10 min and washed with HBSS buffer. Next, Fe2+ probe FerroOrange working solution (1 μM) was added, and the cells were incubated for 30 min in a 37 °C. The intracellular Fe2+ were observed using CLSM.

4T1 cells were seeded into confocal cell culture dishes at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h The cells were then treated with Control, US, CaZC, CaZC+US, CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US for 8 h Next, the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, incubated with the DLAT antibody at 4 °C overnight. The cells were then treated with FITC-labeled goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody at room temperature for 1 h, stained with Actin-Tracker Red and DAPI. The expression levels of DLAT aggregation in 4T1 cells were detected using CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Western blot analysis of cuproptosis and ferroptosis protein
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded into 12-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h Then cells were treated with Control, US, CaZC, CaZC+US, CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US with specified concentrations described above for 8 h After that, the cells were washed by cold PBS twice and added with ice-cold lysis buffer for western blotting. After boiled in loading buffer, each protein sample were separated by SDS-PAGE gels (12 %) and transferred onto polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes. Then, the membranes were blocked with 5 % skim milk powder for 1 h Next, the membrane was incubated with the following primary polyclonal antibodies: FDX-1 (1:1000, Proteintech), LIAS (1:1000, Proteintech), β-Tubulin (1:1000, Proteintech), GPX4 (1:2000, Proteintech), GAPDH (1:2000, Proteintech), respectively, at 4 °C overnight. Then the membranes were washed 3 times with TBST buffer, and incubated with HRP-labeled secondary antibodies (1:5000, Proteintech) for 1 h at room temperature. Finally, protein bands were visualized using an enhanced chemiluminescence system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of immunogenic cell death of 4T1 cells
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were inoculated in confocal dishes and incubated for 24 h The cells were sequentially treated with the medium containing: (1) control; (2) US; (3) CaZC; (4) CaZC+US; (5) CaCuZC; and (6) CaCuZC+US with specified concentrations. The cells in US groups were exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2) for 5 min and incubated for another 12 h After washing with PBS, fixation with 4 % paraformaldehyde, and blockage with 1 % BSA for 20 min, cells were incubated with CRT antibody overnight at 4 °C. After washing with TBST, cells were incubated with FITC-labeled goat anti-rabbit secondary antibody for 40 min at room temperature. After washing with TBST, cells were stained with Hoechst for 10 min and washed with PBS. All the cells were imaged by CLSM. Moreover, the HMGB1 level in supernatant was detected via ELISA kit. The ATP level in the supernatant was detected via ATP assay kit.

The cells were incubated with control and CaCuZC+US, respectively. After washing with PBS, the cells were collected and then added to 2.5 % glutaraldehyde fixative at 4 °C for 24 h The samples were dehydrated, embedded, sliced and measured by bio-TEM.

The 4T1 cells of control group and CaCuZC+US group were collected. According to the manufacturer's instructions, total RNA was extracted from two groups using Trizol. Transcriptome high-throughput sequencing was conducted by Nanjin Personal Gene Technology CO.,LTD.

BALB/c mice were purchased from the Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, P. R. China). The 4T1 tumor-bearing model was prepared by injecting 2.0 × 106 cells in 0.2 mL of FBS free-1640 medium into the BALB/c mice. All animal experiments in this work were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Tianjin University.

Nine healthy female BALB/c mice were randomly divided into three groups to evaluate the biosafety of CaCuZC, including (1) control (saline), (2) 10 mg kg-1 of CaCuZC, (3) 20 mg kg-1 of CaCuZC. Of these, mice of CaCuZC group were intravenously injected with CaCuZC on day 1, 3 and 5. The mice of saline group were injected with same volume of saline. Afterward, blood samples and major organs of mice were collected on 15th day for further blood routine, serum biochemical analysis and H&E staining.

CaCuZC labeled with ICG was intravenously injected into the mice bearing 4T1 tumor via tail vein and the fluorescence imaging signals at different time points (0, 2, 6, 12, and 24 h) were recorded using an IVIS imaging system (NightOWL II LB983, Germany).

When the tumor size reached approximately 100 mm3, the mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 5): (1) control (saline); (2) US (saline plus US irradiation); (3) CaZC (10 mg kg-1); (4) CaZC+US (10 mg kg-1) plus US irradiation; (5) CaCuZC (10 mg kg-1); and (6) CaCuZC (10 mg kg-1) plus US irradiation. These mice received three injections on day 1, 3, and 5. At 24 h post-injection, the mice in US-containing groups were exposed to US irradiation (30 kHz, 2.0 W cm-2, 5 min). The various treatments were repeated three times. Mice weight were recorded every two days. The tumor volume were measured once 2 days, calculated as follows: volume [V] = 1/2 length × width2.

Mice were executed at the end of treatment, and tumors and major organs were obtained from each group. The tumors were weighed and photographed. The collected tumor tissues and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) were analyzed by H&E staining. In addition, terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated dUTP nickend labeling (TUNEL) staining, and Ki-67 immumohistochemical staining were used to observe cellular apoptosis after different treatments.

When the tumor size reached approximately 100 mm3, the mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 3). The drug administration and ultrasound treatment were the same as described above. After treatment, tumors, spleens, and draining lymph nodes were collected and digested to prepare single cell suspensions. After digested with collagenase IV at 37 °C for 1 h, the single cell suspensions could be collected via filtration with a 70 μm mesh. To evaluate the maturation of dendritic cells, cell suspensions from tumor and draining lymph nodes were stained with FITC anti-CD11c, PE anti-CD86 and APC anti-CD80, and evaluated using flow cytometry.

To evaluate the content of CD8+ T cells, cell suspensions from tumor and spleens were stained with FITC anti-CD3, PE anti-CD8a, Cy7 anti-CD45 and APC anti-CD4 antibodies. After that, the content of CD8+ T cells were qualified by flow cytometry. All above antibodies were purchased from BioLegend. In addition, blood was collected from mice and serum was collected by centrifugation, and cytokines (IL-2, TNF-α) were detected using corresponding ELISA kits.

All experimental data were analyzed using Origin 2019 and GraphPad Prism 8.0, and the mean standard deviation (SD) was generated. The significance of the data was evaluated according to the one-way ANOVA test or two-way ANOVA. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

The procedure for preparing the CaO2@CuZIF/CD (CaCuZC) is shown in Scheme 1A. The CaO2 nanoparticles, which were prepared by the wet chemical method, exhibited a regular morphology with uniform size in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Figs. 1 a and S1). The nanosonosensitizer IR780 CD was prepared by solvothermal approach using IR780 iodide molecules as precursor. The TEM images of IR780 CD showed a uniform distribution with an average diameter of approximately 5 nm (Fig. 1b). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of IR780 CD was blue-shifted compared to IR-780 iodide, further indicating successful synthesis of IR780 CD (Fig. S4a). Next, the CaO2@CuZIF/CD (CaCuZC) nanoreactor were obtained by the self-assembly process of Zn2+and 2-methylimidazole, encapsulating the nanosonosensitizer IR780 CD, the Fenton-like reagent Cu2+, and CaO2 NPs. The encapsulation rate of IR780 CD in the CaCuZC was quantitatively analyzed to be as high up to 52.3 % through the standard calibration curve of IR780 CD. As revealed by TEM, the obtained CaCuZC exhibited a uniform morphology with a mean diameter of approximately 192 nm (Fig. 1c). Elemental mapping demonstrated that the C, N, O, Zn, Cu and Ca elements were homogeneously distributed in the CaCuZC (Fig. 1d). The average particle size of CaCuZC was about 195.2 nm as measured by dynamic light scattering, and the zeta potentials of CaO2 and CaCuZC were +15.7 mV and −19.5 mV, respectively (Fig. 1e and f). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CaCuZC expressed the characteristic absorption peak of IR780 CD at 540 nm, indicating that the nanosonosensitizer IR780 CD was successfully encapsulated in the CaCuZC (Fig. 1g).The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CaCuZC displayed typical peaks associated with C 1 s, O 1 s, Ca 2p, Cu 2p, and Zn 2p (Fig. 1h). The high-resolution XPS analysis of Cu 2p in CaCuZC indicated the existence of Cu2+ (Fig. 1i). The Ca 2p spectrum showed that the signals at 350.12 eV and 347.38 eV, which were attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of calcium, respectively (Fig. 1j). Besides, the peaks in the high-resolution spectrum of Zn 2p can be assigned to 2p1/2, 2p3/2 of Zn 2p (Fig. S2). Nitrogen adsorption/desorption experiments showed type I isotherms for CaCuZC, suggesting the porous structure of CaCuZC (Fig. S3). During 9 days of storage, CaCuZC maintained good dispersion in water, PBS, and RPMI media with 10 % serum, indicating that CaCuZC would have good stability during long-term circulation (Fig. S4).

To evaluate the sonodynamic activity of CaCuZC containing IR780 CD, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used to measure singlet oxygen (1O2) production. In the presence of 1O2, DPBF decomposed into 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene and its fluorescence was quenched. As shown in Fig. S12, the DPBF+US group as control, showed a slight decrease in absorbance under US irradiation. In sharp contrast, the characteristic absorption of IR780 CD + US group markedly decreased during ultrasound treatment (30 kHz, 1.5 W cm-2), demonstrating that US triggered the generation of 1O2 (Fig. S13a). After IR780 CD was encapsulated into the CaCuZC, the ability of CaCuZC to generate ROS was also examined (Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, the 1O2 production of CaCuZC under US irradiation was measured by singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) probe. In the absence of IR780 CD, the fluorescence intensitiy of SOSG slightly increased with US irradiation (Fig. S14). However, the fluorescence intensity of SOSG solution containing IR780 CD or CaCuZC clearly increased gradually under US irradiation, indicating CaCuZC had the satisfactory efficiency of SDT under US irradiation (Figs. 2b and S13b).

GSH, which is an overexpressed antioxidant in tumor cells, demonstrates a robust scavenging impact on ROS and weakens the efficacy of ROS-based cancer therapy. Fortunately, GSH is depleted by CaCuZC to generate glutathione disulfide, which enhances the SDT/CDT effect. Thus, 5,5-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) was used as a probe to investigate its GSH depletion effect. The principle is that DTNB reacts with the -SH group of GSH to form the 2-nitro-5-thiobenzoate anion (TNB2-),exhibiting a bright yellow color at 412 nm. Firstly, it was demonstrated that Cu2+ could deplete GSH by oxidation. The characteristic absorbance of DTNB gradually decreased with concentration of Cu2+ increasing, indicating that Cu2+effectively decreased the GSH level by the reduction reaction between Cu2+ and GSH (Fig. S10). Next, the ability of CaCuZC to deplete GSH was validated (Fig. 2c). According to the standard curves (Fig. S9), the amount of GSH consumed was as high as 82.1 % when the concentration of CaCuZC was 1.25 mg mL-1, demonstrating that CaCuZC effectively depleted GSH and reduced the concentration of GSH (Fig. S11).

In addition to sonodynamic activity, the chemodynamic efficacy of CaCuZC was also assessed. Compared with the Fenton reaction catalyzed by Fe(II), Cu(I) is more likely to initiate Fenton-like reactions in terms of chemical kinetics and can proceed efficiently even under neutral conditions. The generation of hydroxyl radicals was first investigated by using methylene blue (MB) as an indicator. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, neither GSH+H2O2 nor CaO2@ZIF/CD (CaZC)+GSH+H2O2 groups generated hydroxyl radicals, whereas CaO2@CuZIF/CD (CaCuZC)+GSH and CaCuZC+GSH+H2O2 groups caused the absorbance of MB to significantly decrease, indicating that the simultaneous presence of Cu2+ and H2O2 was required for the Fenton-like reaction to generate hydroxyl radicals. In the CaCuZC, GSH, and H2O2 reaction system (pH 5.0), the absorption peak of MB gradually decreased over the reaction time, indicating that the reaction system continued to produce hydroxyl radicals (Fig. 2e). Furthermore, increase in the CaCuZC or H2O2 concentration could significantly improve the rate of MB degradation (Fig. 2f and g), implying that the ROS originated from the Fenton-like reaction between H2O2 and CaCuZC. Moreover, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was used as a probe for the detection of hydroxyl radicals. Unlike MB, the reaction between TMB and hydroxyl radicals resulted in an obvious color change from colorless to blue (Fig. S15a). As shown in Fig. 2h, CaCuZC+GSH and CaCuZC+GSH+H2O2 groups produced a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm, and TMB oxidation was clearly found, which was attributed to the Fenton-like reaction induced by CaCuZC. The other groups containing H2O2 or CaO2@ZIF/CD (CaZC) remained colorless due to the absence of the Fenton-like reaction. As shown in Fig. 2i, the absorption peak of TMB at 652 nm gradually increased with reaction time. Furthermore, the TMB oxidation process positively correlated with the CaCuZC and H2O2 concentrations, which was ascribed to ROS originating from the Fenton-like reaction between H2O2 and CaCuZC (Figs. 2j and S15b). To further clarify the sonodynamic and chemodynamic behavior, the electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra was employed to ascertain the generation of ROS. The triplet ESR signals couldn't be detected in the CaCuZC group, but the ESR spectra of CaCuZC+US group revealed a typical peak pattern of 1O2 under irradiation, which suggested the generation of 1O2(Fig. 2k). The typical four-fold peaks of the generated hydroxyl appeared in CaCuZC+GSH+H2O2 group; however, no clear signals were observed in Control group (Fig. 2l). The ESR spectra were consistent with the tendency shown by UV–vis spectrophotometry.

To verify the pH-responsive biodegradation, CaCuZC were dispersed into phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solutions with different pH values (7.4, 6.5 and 5.0). The release profile of IR780 CD, Ca2+ and H2O2 from CaCuZC were evaluated by UV–vis spectroscopy. Within 24 h, the cumulative release rates of IR780 CD reached 78.1 % at pH 5.0 (Fig. S5c), based on the linear relationship between the IR780 CD concentration and UV–vis absorbance (Fig. S5b and d), which were significantly higher than those in the other groups. At pH 7.4, Ca2+ was only minimally released, while the proportion of released Ca2+ was 81.2 % after 12 h of incubation in PBS at pH 5.0, indicating that CaCuZC undergoes acid-responsive decomposition (Fig. S6). CaO2 reacted with water to form O2 or H2O2, and the in vitro H2O2 production during CaCuZC degradation was evaluated. The titanium sulfate method was employed to measure the ability of CaCuZC to produce H2O2 under weakly acidic condition. The principle is that when a titanium salt solution reacts with H2O2, a yellow peroxide-titanium complex appears. No absorption peaks appeared when the reaction system contained PBS, while characteristic absorption peaks appeared when it contained H2O2, CaO2, and CaCuZC (Fig. S7). Within 24 h, the release of H2O2 reached 83.3 % at pH 5.0 (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro therapeutic efficacy of CaCuZC
ce:section.ce:para: Before evaluating the effect of treatment at the cellular level, the cell uptake efficiency of CaCuZC in 4T1 cells was first evaluated using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). For fluorescence tracking, CaCuZC were first labeled with the fluorescent dye rhodamine B (RhB). After incubation with RhB-labeled CaCuZC, the red fluorescence intensity in 4T1 cells was observed at different time points (1, 3, and 5 h). The intracellular red fluorescence was significantly enhanced with an increasing incubation time, indicating that CaCuZC was effectively endocytosed by 4T1 cells (Fig. 3 d).

To investigate the intracellular ROS level, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was used to detect the intracellular ROS levels in each group. DCFH-DA is oxidized into highly fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) molecules by intracellular ROS. The synergistic therapeutic efficacy was evaluated using 4T1 cells with 6 groups as follows: Control, Ultrasound (US), CaO2@ZIF/CD (CaZC), CaZC+US, CaO2@CuZIF/CD (CaCuZC), and CaCuZC+US groups (Fig. 3e). No green fluorescence was observed in the Control or US groups, and a slight green fluorescence was observed in the CaZC group because Ca2+ and H2O2 released by CaZC increased the intracellular level of oxidative stress. Then, the CaZC+US group showed significant green fluorescence, indicating that IR780 CD produced ROS under US irradiation. A visible green fluorescence appeared in the CaCuZC group, which was because of Cu2+-mediated Fenton-like reaction. Moreover, the green fluorescence intensity in the CaCuZC+US group was significantly higher than that in the group treated with CaZC+US (single SDT) or CaCuZC (single CDT), which was because of the synergistic effect of CDT and SDT enabled by CaCuZC+US.

Next, the killing effect of CaCuZC at the cellular level was evaluated by a cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. As shown in Fig. 3a, the cell survival rate was 72.3 % when the CaZC concentration reached 120 μg mL-1, because Ca2+ and H2O2 from CaZC caused oxidative stress that killed tumor cells. After US irradiation, CaZC treatment resulted in a significant decrease in cell survival, which was attributed to ROS generated by IR780 CD. As shown in Fig. 3b, the cell viability decreased continuously from 95 % to 37.3 % with increasing CaCuZC concentrations because the CaCuZC-mediated cuproptosis–ferroptosis-CDT and Ca2+ overload kill tumor cells. Compared to the other groups, CaCuZC+US group exhibited the highest degree of toxicity to 4T1 cells, which was attributed to combined effect of cuproptosis- ferroptosis enhanced-SDT/CDT and Ca2+ overload (Fig. 3c).

The cell-killing effect in various treatment groups was evaluated by a live/dead staining assay. The results of Calcein-AM (green) and Propidium iodide (red) co-staining of 4T1 cells in the different treatment groups are shown in Fig. 3f. The 4T1 cells in the control and US groups showed green fluorescence, indicating live cells. Most of the cells in the CaZC group showed green fluorescence, with a small number showing red fluorescence. The slight cell-killing effect of CaZC was attributed to oxidative stress induced by calcium overload and accumulation of H2O2. When US irradiation was applied, red fluorescence which represents dead cells, appeared in the CaZC+US group. With US irradiation, IR780 CD generates large amounts of 1O2 in 4T1 cells and calcium overload amplifies oxidative stress to induce apoptosis. Then, obvious red fluorescence was also observed in the CaCuZC group, due to cuproptosis–ferroptosis/calcium overload enhanced CDT. In the CaCuZC+US group, almost all cells showed red fluorescence, indicating the lowest survival rate of tumor cells in this group. The synergistic effect of cuproptosis–ferroptosis/Ca2+ overload enhanced CDT-SDT resulted in the most severe apoptosis.

In addition, the apoptosis of cells was quantitatively investigated by flow cytometry by using an Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis staining kit. The basic principle is that phosphatidylserine flips to the outer membrane during apoptosis, which can be specifically recognized by green fluorescent Annexin V-FITC, while red fluorescent propidium iodide (PI) can cross the broken cell membrane of dead cells, allowing the nucleus to be stained red. The percentage of viable cells in the control and US groups was about 96 %, and the apoptosis rates in the CaZC, CaZC+US, and CaCuZC groups were 23.0, 64.8, and 78.0 %, respectively. The CaCuZC+US group had the highest apoptosis rate of 93.1 % due to the synergistic cell-killing effects of cuproptosis–ferroptosis/Ca2+ overload enhanced-SDT/CDT(Fig. 3g).

Inspired by the in vitro antitumor effect, the effect mechanism of CaCuZC-induced cell death was explored in detail. A schematic representation illustrates the synergistic cuproptosis–ferroptosis therapy (Fig. 4 a). Several complementary approaches confirmed the proposed molecular mechanism. As the most important endogenous antioxidant in tumor cells, GSH is significantly involved in cuproptosis, ferroptosis and apoptosis. Considering that GSH can be converted to oxidized glutathione by Cu2+, the GSH-depleting capacity of CaCuZC may result in cuproptosis and ferroptosis. In addition, the intracellular GSH depletion enhanced the killing effect of ROS on tumor cells by disrupting redox homeostasis. Therefore, the intracellular GSH depletion by different materials was first investigated using a commercial intracellular GSH assay kit. Compared with the control group, the GSH level in cells treated with CaZC+US was moderately reduced, which may be due to the increased oxidative stress caused by CaZC+US (Fig. 4b). CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US groups presented the significant reduction in intracellular GSH content, which was attributed to the simultaneous ROS generation by SDT/CDT and Cu2+-induced GSH depletion.

In the cuproptosis process, FDX1 is a key enzyme and has a strong reducing ability to lipoylate DLAT. Cu2+ released from the nanomaterials are first reduced to Cu+ by FDX 1, which induces lipoylated DLAT aggregation and degradation of iron-sulfur cluster proteins, leading to proteotoxic stress and eventual cell death. FDX 1 and lipoyl synthase (LIAS) are upstream regulatory factors for protein acylation, and their dysregulation can promote the reduction of Fe-S cluster proteins. Change in FDX 1 and LIAS were assessed using western blot. As shown in Fig. 4c, Fe-S cluster proteins FDX1 and LIAS were downregulated in CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US treatments. The levels of FDX1 and LIAS in CaCuZC+US group were 0.51-fold and 0.71-fold of those in control group, respectively (Fig. 4d). Since these two proteins play a key regulatory role in cuproptosis, their reduced expression demonstrates that treatment with CaCuZC leads to proteotoxic stress and cell death. Next, we investigated DLAT oligomerization using immunofluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 4e, the cells treated with Control, US, CaZC, and CaZC+US displayed negligible DLAT foci, while the cells treated with CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US showed significant DLAT foci.

Furthermore, intracellular GSH depletion could lead to significant down-regulation of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) expression and lipid peroxidation (LPO) and ferrous ions accumulation, which efficiently induced ferroptosis in cancer cells (Fig. 4a). As shown in Fig. 4c, GPX4 was significantly downregulated after cells were treated with CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US. The level of GPX4 in CaCuZC+US group was 0.56-fold of that in control group (Fig. 4d). As one of the key biomarkers of ferroptosis, LPO was then detected by using C11-BODIPY as a ratiometric fluorescent indicator. When C11-BODIPY is oxidized, its fluorescence will change from red to green. CaCuZC+US-treated cells exhibited the brightest green fluorescence and the darkest red fluorescence, suggesting the highest level in lipid peroxidation (Fig. 4f). In addition, another ferroptosis biomarker, intracellular Fe2+level was detected using the Fe2+ probe FerroOrange. The cellular Fe2+ content was significantly increased in the CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US groups (Fig. 4g). The above results demonstrate the contribution of ferroptosis to CaCuZC-induced cell death.

Considering calcium ion (Ca2+) overload could damage mitochondria, the intracellular Ca2+ level was analyzed by using Ca2+ probe Fluo-3AM. No visible green fluorescence was observed in the Control or US groups; however, the CaZC, CaZC+US, CaCuZC, and CaCuZC+US groups showed bright green fluorescence, indicating that free calcium ions from CaO2 decomposition effectively increased the intracellular Ca2+ concentration (Fig. 5 a). In addition, the intracellular Ca2+ level of each group was also quantified by flow cytometry, and the results corresponded to the CLSM. The calcium ion levels of CaZC, CaZC+US, CaCuZC and CaCuZC+US groups were significantly higher than those of the Control and US groups (Fig. 5b).

Mitochondria as key organelles in cells are involved in many cellular activities, such as energy supply, calcium homeostasis, and lipid metabolism. Both intracellular Ca2+/Cu2+overload and the large accumulation of ROS could lead to mitochondrial membrane depolarization and subsequent cuproptosis–ferroptosis. To verify this process, the mitochondrial membrane potential of 4T1 cells after various treatments was assessed using JC-10 assay kit. As a cationic probe, JC-10 aggregates in the mitochondrial matrix at a high mitochondrial membrane potential (normal mitochondria), forming a polymer and producing red fluorescence; however, JC-10 remains in a green fluorescence monomeric form at a low mitochondrial membrane potential. As shown in Fig. 5c, 4T1 cells in the Control and US groups showed red fluorescence, indicating a high mitochondrial membrane potential and normal cell status. Compared with those in the Control, US, and CaZC groups, 4T1 cells treated with CaZC+US showed visible green fluorescence, confirming that ROS generation and Ca2+ overload induced mitochondrial dysfunction. CaCuZC+US-treated 4T1 cells showed the strongest green fluorescence, which was attributed to the mitochondrial damage by cuproptosis–ferroptosis-Ca2+overload enhanced SDT/CDT. To investigate the specific morphological changes, mitochondria subjected to different treatments were observed using bio-TEM. Many morphologically normal mitochondria were observed in Control-4T1 cells; however, the breakdown of the outer mitochondrial membrane, reduction in size, and decrease or disappearance of cristae could be observed in CaCuZC+US-treated cells, which were consistent with the main features of cells suffering cuproptosis–ferroptosis-Ca2+overload (Fig. 5d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro ICD induced by CaCuZC
ce:section.ce:para: ICD has become a promising method for activating anti-tumor immunity and transforming a “cold” tumor into a “hot” tumor. To investigate whether ICD could be triggered to promote the recruitment and maturation of antigen-presenting cells, exposure or release of multiple DAMPs was studied at the cellular level. CRT, HMGB1, and ATP are typical DAMPs that were chosen as indicators to assess the ICD effect. The exposure of CRT on the plasma membrane of 4T1 cells was observed by immunofluorescence staining (Fig. 5e). The cells in the control, US, and CaZC groups did not show green fluorescence; however, visible green fluorescence was observed in the CaZC+US, CaCuZC, and CaCuZC+US groups, indicating that CRT was exposed to the cell membrane surface. The release of high mobility group box protein 1 (HMGB l) into the extracellular matrix activated the maturation of dendritic cells and triggered the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines. The release level of HMGB 1 from the nucleus to the extracellular space was measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The CaZC+US, CaCuZC, and CaCuZC+US groups showed significantly higher HMGB1 release than the Control and US groups (Fig. 5f). Extracellular ATP acts as another DAMP signal, which induces chemotactic activity and enables NLRP3 inflammasome maturation in macrophages. Next, the level of ATP secreted from the dead cells was measured. The ATP content after CaCuZC+US treatment was higher than that of the Control and US groups (Fig. 5g). Overall, cuproptosis–ferroptosis and calcium overload enhanced immunotherapy effectively triggered ICD by enhancing DAMP exposure and release.

To elucidate the underlying molecular mechanism by which CaCuZC+US killing cancer cells, we performed a comparative RNA-seq analysis of the 4T1 cell transcriptomes. Three biological replicates were conducted for each combination. The comparison of the RNA expression profiles between CaCuZC+US treated group against the control group have been shown in Fig. S16. The difference of expression level between two samples (Fold change or FC) is expressed as Log2 transformed ratio. The Log2 value≥1.5 was used as a cutoff to select differentially expressed transcripts.

The results showed that in the CaCuZC+US treated group, the expression was induced for 2804 genes and was repressed for 2222 genes. Principal component analysis revealed significant differences in gene expression between the control and CaCuZC+US groups (Fig. S17). Compared with the control group, a total of 7552 genes were significantly regulated in the CaCuZC+US group. With the cut-off set at P < 0.05 and |log2[fold change]|≥2, 4322 upregulated genes and 3230 downregulated genes were identified, as shown in a volcano plot (Fig. 6 a). Some of the DEGs related to apoptosis were screened for clustering (Fig. 6b). The treatment with CaCuZC+US significantly upregulated or downregulated the expression of apoptosis related genes in tumor cells, such as tumor necrosis factor and caspase, which are closely related to apoptosis. To explore the biological process (BP) of the DEGs enrichment after CaCuZC+US treatment, gene ontology (GO) analysis was conducted (Fig. 6c). It was shown that the DEGs were most involved in cell differentiation, immune system process, positive regulation of apoptotic process, cellular response to calcium ion and cell redox homeostasis. Furthermore, the DEGs pathway enrichment were analyzed through the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database. As shown in Fig. 6d, Metabolic pathways, Pathways in cancer, FoxO signaling pathway, TNF signaling pathway and Apoptosis signaling pathway was mostly gathered in the CaCuZC+US treated group. This suggests that the CaCuZC+US treatment could exert the therapeutic effect by disturbing the expression of apoptosis and Ca2+ overload related genes in cells.

Among the up-regulated genes in the CaCuZC+US treated 4T1 cells, three functional groups were particularly enriched. One group contained 11 Ferroptosis proteins, including Hmox1, Rnp, Mao1lc3b, Ftl1, Map1lc3a, Tfrc, Fth1, Pcbp1, Slc39a8, Gclm and Sat1. The second group contained 26 Apoptosis proteins, including Hrk, Pik3r3, Ctsw, Csf2rb, Tuba3a, Bax, etc. The third group contained 24 FoxO signaling pathway proteins, including Gabarapl2, Plk3, Tgfb2, Gabarapl1, Cdkn1a, G6 pc, etc.

Among the down-regulated genes in the CaCuZC+US treated 4T1 cells, the genes of four functional groups were particularly enriched. One group contains 26 Hippo signaling pathway proteins, including Patj, Prkcz, Stk3, Wnt6, Ccnd1, Tgfb1, Birc3, etc. The second group contained 25 breast cancer proteins that included Wnt10b, Tcf7l1, Fzd2, Fzd4, Fzd7, Notch4, Wnt7b, and few others. The third group contained 21 HIF-1 signaling pathway genes, including Egln3, Cdkn1b, Nos2, Ifngr1, Ifngr2, Insr, Cul2, etc. And the fourth group contained 16 Th1 and Th2 cell differentiation proteins, including Stat5a, Stat5b, Ifngr1, Ifngr2, Il4ra, Il4, Jak2, Nfkbie, Jak3, and few others.

These results indicate that CaCuZC+US treatment induces tumor cell death by acting on Ferroptosis, Apoptosis, FoxO signaling, Hippo signaling, HIF-1 signaling pathways, and Th1 and Th2 cell differentiation related proteins. It forms a multiple protein network that systematically kills tumor cells.

Before studying the in vivo therapeutic effects of CaCuZC, the biocompatibility and safety in vivo were first evaluated. Nine healthy BALB/c mice were randomly divided into three groups (n = 3): (1) a control group with an intravenous injection of saline, and (2) experimental groups with an intravenous injection of CaCuZC (10 mg kg-1 or 20 mg kg-1). The mice were sacrificed to collect blood samples and major organs. Blood indices were found to be within the normal reference range according to routine blood tests, demonstrating the hemocompatibility of CaCuZC (Fig. S18). Then, blood biochemistry analysis of aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (CRE), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and creatine kinase (CK), which reflect liver and kidney function, was performed. Serum biochemical analysis indicated that CaCuZC exhibited high biosafety (Fig. S19). H&E staining of the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) was performed. Compared with the control group, no significant pathological abnormalities or inflammation were observed in the major organs of the experimental mice (Fig. S20), indicating that CaCuZC demonstrated good biosafety at the selected concentrations.

Inspired by the therapeutic effect of CaCuZC in vitro, the in vivo antitumor effect of CaCuZC was further investigated using the 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model. Before treatment, the tumor accumulation of the CaCuZC was assessed by NIR fluorescence imaging. The CaCuZC were labeled with near-infrared dye ICG, and intravenously injected into tumor-bearing mice. The fluorescence images and the corresponding quantitative results indicated that CaCuZC could effectively accumulate at the tumor site and stay for a long time (Fig. S21). The experimental protocol is shown in Fig. 7 a. Seven days prior to treatment, 4T1 cells were inoculated subcutaneously into the right abdomen of the mice. When the tumor volume reached around 100 mm3, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 5): (1) Control, (2) US, (3) CaZC, (4) CaZC+US, (5) CaCuZC, and (6) CaCuZC+US groups. Then, 24 h after injection of CaZC or CaCuZC, tumors were irradiated with US (30 kHz, 2.0 W cm-2, 5 min). The various treatments were repeated three times. During the treatment period, the tumor volume and mouse body weight were measured every 2 days, and the tumor growth curve and body weight changes were plotted. It was found that the tumor volumes increased continuously in the control, US, and CaZC groups. Compared with the control group, the tumor growth rate did not decrease significantly after ultrasound treatment, indicating that ultrasound alone had almost no effect on tumor growth (Fig. 7b). Tumor growth was significantly inhibited in the CaZC+US group, demonstrating that IR780 CD mediated-SDT and calcium overload inhibited tumor growth. Tumor growth was also more inhibited in the CaCuZC group, relying on Cu2+-mediated CDT/cuproptosis–ferroptosis and calcium overload. The most significant tumor growth inhibition was observed in the CaCuZC+US group due to the high synergistic enhanced efficacy of cuproptosis–ferroptosis-calcium overload and SDT/CDT. After treatment, the tumors of the mice were weighed (Fig. 7c). Based on the above data, the tumor growth inhibition rate was calculated. A high inhibition rate of 68.1 % was achieved in the CaCuZC+US group compared to the CaZC+US (50.6 %) and CaCuZC (56.7 %) groups (Fig. 7d). Compared with the Control and US groups, the tumor size and weight in the CaZC+US, CaCuZC, and CaCuZC+US groups were significantly reduced, indicating that the nanomaterials had significant therapeutic effect. Furthermore, the body weight of mice in all groups increased slightly during the treatment period, indicating that CaCuZC had good biosafety (Fig. S22).

The therapeutic effect of each group was further analyzed by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, and immunohistochemical staining with TdT-mediated dUTP nick-end labeling (TUNEL) and Ki-67 (Fig. 7e). H&E staining images revealed that the morphology of the cells was normal with clearly distinguishable membranes and nuclear structures in the Control, US, and CaZC groups. Bulky cavities and a large amount of overflowing cytoplasm appeared in the tumor tissue of the CaZC+US, CaCuZC, and CaCuZC+US groups, indicating apoptosis or necrosis of tumor cells. TUNEL staining indicated apoptosis in the tumor sections, which was consistent with the H&E staining data. Tumors in the CaCuZC+US group exhibited the most pronounced cellular necrosis or damage. In addition, the proliferative bioactivity of cancer cells was detected by Ki-67 immunohistochemical staining. A positive nuclear signal with strong dark brown color (indicating proliferating tumor cells) was observed in the control, US, and CaZC groups. In contrast, the number of Ki-67-positive cells was significantly reduced in the CaCuZC+US group. In addition, H&E staining of the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) was performed for histomorphometric analysis at the end of the treatment (Fig. S23). No significant damage or inflammation was observed in the tissue sections, indicating that CaCuZC had high histocompatibility during treatment.

ICD has emerged as a promising approach for activating anti-tumor immunity, transforming cold tumors into hot tumors. CD80 and CD86, as important molecules, provide co-stimulatory signals and significantly regulate T cell activation. T lymphocytes are the key mediators of tumor destruction with high specificity for tumor-expressed antigens. To evaluate the in vivo immune response induced by ICD, DC maturation in tumors and tumor-draining lymph nodes were assessed using flow cytometry, due to their important role in antigen presentation and T lymphocyte priming and activation. The populations of mature DCs (CD80+CD86+ DCs) in the tumor in the control and US groups were less than 19 %, demonstrating that saline or ultrasound treatment had little effect on DC maturity (Fig. 8 a and e). The percentages of mature DCs in the CaZC group and CaZC+US group were higher than the control group, and greatest percentage of mature DCs in the CaCuZC+US group was observed due to cuproptosis–ferroptosis-Ca2+overload enhanced SDT/CDT. In addition, the proportion of mature DCs in the tumor-draining lymph nodes was also analyzed, and a similar trend was observed (Fig. 8b and f). The low percentages of mature DCs (CD80+CD86+ DCs) in the control and US groups indicated maturation of DCs was not significantly affected by the treatments of saline or US irradiation. However, the CaZC+US group and CaCuZC+US group mature DCs displayed a rise in the numbers of matured DCs, compared with control group. These results illustrated that the combined treatment modality based on CaCuZC contributed to a remarkable maturation of DCs.

It is widely acknowledged that CD8+ T cells are the main effector cells in the anti-tumour immune response, recognising and destroying cancer cells and thus preventing tumour growth. Thus, the upregulation of CD8+ T cells represents a direct manifestation of systemic immune response activation. Afterward, the percentage of activated CD8+ T cells in the tumor and spleen in the various groups was further quantified using flow cytometry. The CD8+ T cell assay results of the tumors are shown in Fig. 8c and g. The results of flow cytometry showed that the percentage of CD8+ T cells was significantly increased in both the CaZC+US and CaCuZC groups, and were 1.6-fold and 2.3-fold higher than that of the control group, respectively, demonstrating moderate immune activation. The CaCuZC+US treatment produced the highest proportion of CD8+ T cells in the tumors, revealing its strong immune effector cell activation capacity due to cuproptosis–ferroptosis-Ca2+overload enhanced SDT/CDT. Next, the content of CD8+ T cells in the spleen was also analyzed (Fig. 8d and h). Specifically, the percentage of CD8+ T cells in the CaZC+US and CaCuZC+US groups were significantly higher compared to the control group, with a 1.4-fold and 1.6-fold increase, respectively. Apparently, CaCuZC-based cuproptosis–ferroptosis and immunotherapy synergistically promoted the priming, amplification, and infiltration of cytotoxic T lymphocytes to attack tumor cells. Besides, cytokines play an integral role in the development and differentiation of immune cells, immune responses, and regulation. Several immune-related cytokines promote DC maturation, antigen presentation, and T cell proliferation, thus playing a key role in the anti-tumor immune response. To further investigate the anti-tumor immunity of CaZC and CaCuZC, the levels of immune-related cytokines in the serum of different treatment groups were assessed to further confirm the activation of the immune response. The levels of tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α) and interleukin 2 (IL-2) in the CaCuZC+US group were 1.72- and 2.36-fold higher than those in the control group, respectively, showing the strongest immune response. It indicated that the combination therapy was effective in promoting the secretion of cytokines, stimulating cytotoxic T lymphocytes, and activating anti-tumor immunity to a certain extent (Fig. 7f and g). The nanomedicine CaCuZC based on cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload effectively activated immune responses to attack tumor cells via recruiting tumor-attacking immune cells. Overall, these results demonstrated that cuproptosis-ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload enhanced immunotherapy effectively induced DCs maturation promoted the proliferation and activation of CD8+ T cells as well as activated anti-tumor immune response.

In summary, a cascade nanoreactor CaCuZC was successfully constructed based on a self-assembly strategy for cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload mediated immunotherapy. Upon decomposition within the acidic and GSH-overexpressing tumor microenvironment, CaCuZC released CaO2 and Cu2+ and sonosensitizer IR780 CD. The Cu2+ released from CaCuZC decomposition was converted to Cu+ by GSH and generated hydroxyl radicals via Fenton-like reaction. The unprotected CaO2 further produced H2O2/O2 and Ca2+ in a weakly acidic environment to strengthen the Cu2+-based CDT and IR780 CD-mediated SDT, respectively. The overload copper ions not only led to cuproptosis by inducing DLAT aggregation, but also efficiently induced ferroptosis via GPX4 inactivation and LPO accumulation. In addition, Ca2+ overload amplified intracellular oxidative stress and cuased mitochondrial dysfunction, activating antitumor immune response by inducing immunogenic cell death. The combination of Cu2+and Ca2+ overload induced cascade mitochondrial damage. Given that several studies have identified the correlation between mitochondria dysfunction and tumor immune response, and cuproptosis as a mitochondria respiration-dependent cell death pathway, this enhanced approach to cuproptosis–ferroptosis is expected to be further examined in future studies based on immunotherapy and mitochondrial regulation. This study provides a promising approach for cuproptosis–ferroptosis and Ca2+ overload mediated immunotherapy.
